one_line_takeaway: VARP improves VLM-labeled preference RL for robots by overlaying trajectory sketches for richer temporal feedback and adding an agent-return regularizer that keeps the learned reward aligned with the current policy, raising preference accuracy (68%->84%) and MetaWorld success (<50%->~80%) while reducing reward hacking.
key_finding: Overlaying trajectory sketches raises VLM preference accuracy on near-identical end states from 68% (final image alone) to 84%, with the largest gains when the two trajectories have similar rewards. Combining sketches with agent-preference regularization lifts MetaWorld success rates from below 50% for standard VLM-based approaches to roughly 70-80%, increases episode rewards by about 27%, and reduces a reward-misalignment metric from 0.35 to 0.15, curbing reward hacking and stabilizing training toward oracle-level behavior.
core_contribution: VARP (VLM + Agent Regularized Preferences) is a preference-based RL pipeline for continuous-control robotics with two distinct novel components. (1) Trajectory-aware preference labeling: instead of showing a Vision-Language Model a single final-state image (as in the prior method RL-VLM-F), VARP projects the robot's full end-effector trajectory onto the camera's 2D plane using known camera parameters and overlays it as a color-coded sketch (hue shifting from bright yellow at the start to darker at the end) on the final frame, giving the VLM temporal context to compare motions that yield near-identical end states. (2) Agent-regularized reward learning: VARP augments the standard Bradley-Terry preference loss with an agent-preference regularizer L_agent(nu)=lambda*E_{tau~pi_theta}[-R(tau)] that penalizes reward functions assigning high value to low-return trajectories under the current policy, framed as a first-order approximation to a bilevel optimization that couples reward learning with policy optimization. These are VARP's own additions and are explicitly distinguished from the cited PEBBLE, RIME, SURF, Christiano et al., and RL-VLM-F prior work in the Related Work section.
threat_model: N/A. This is a robotics reward-learning methods paper, not an AI-security paper. There is no adversary, attack surface, or threat model. The paper does address 'reward hacking' (the policy discovering undesirable shortcuts that exploit a misspecified learned reward), which is an AI-safety/robustness concern rather than an adversarial-security one; the authors treat it as a stability/alignment problem to be mitigated by agent-preference regularization, not as a defense against a malicious actor. No security threat model is invented here per the paper's own framing.
limitations: Effectiveness is upper-bounded by the underlying VLM, which can still mislabel complex scenes. Generating 2D trajectory sketches adds modest overhead and can be inaccurate under occlusions or unusual camera viewpoints (the simpler pixel-tracking alternative for the end-effector can also produce inaccurate sketches). Tasks with many sub-goals or very long horizons may need more granular feedback; supplying additional intermediate images or subgoal annotations would help but requires many more VLM queries and a larger replay buffer, potentially negating the efficiency gains. Reported quantitative gains come from figure-based curves on a small set of tasks (three MetaWorld, two DMControl); no confidence intervals or seed counts are stated in the paper, and several headline numbers (approx 15-20%, 20-30%, 27%) are approximate.

datasets_used: MetaWorld manipulation tasks: Drawer Open, Soccer, Sweep Into
quantitative_results: VLM preference accuracy on near-identical end states: 68% with final image alone vs 84% with trajectory sketches
Trajectory sketches improve preference accuracy by approximately 15-20% on MetaWorld (abstract)
MetaWorld success rate: below 50% for standard approaches vs ~70-80% for VARP
Episode rewards increase by about 27% when combining sketches and agent preference
Agent-aware regularization boosts episode returns by 20-30% in locomotion tasks (abstract)
Reward-misalignment metric reduced from 0.35 to 0.15 with agent-preference regularization
baselines_compared: RL-VLM-F / VLM Preference (single final-state image, two-stage prompting, labels -1/0/1) [Wang et al. 2024]
VLM Score (VLM gives a direct numeric score 0-1 per observation instead of pairwise preference)
Ground-Truth (GT) preference oracle from each environment's built-in reward (upper bound)
PEBBLE and SURF with a scripted teacher (used in the agent-regularization ablation)
